sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Julia Jones, a 25-year-old female, presents for evaluation of breast reduction due to symptomatic macromastia.
She has been experiencing back pain, neck pain, and discomfort due to her breast size, which has been a concern for about 7 years.
She has tried nonsurgical treatments, including chiropractic care, with minimal relief.
She is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty.
She is currently a student, studying psychology, and has a family history of breast cancer, with her mother diagnosed at age 40.
She is otherwise healthy, having lost 10 pounds in the past few months and plans to lose an additional 20 pounds.
Past medical history: The patient has a family history of breast cancer, with her mother diagnosed at age 40.
She has not had any mammograms, but plans to start at age 30.
She does not have any known allergies.
Review of systems: The patient reports back pain, neck pain, and discomfort due to her breast size, which improves with bra use.
She experiences numbness and tingling in her hands and occasional nipple pain.
She has not experienced any rashes or discharge.
Current medications: She takes propranolol for migraines.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: Inspection reveals asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally.
There is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally.
No breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Julia Jones is a 25-year-old female with symptomatic macromastia, ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty.
Problem list: Macromastia; Active.
Plan:
I prescribed no medications.
I will submit the documentation and photos to the insurance company for approval.
Once approved, we will schedule the surgery and meet again to discuss the procedure and answer any questions.
The patient will need to have a clear plastic tape over the incision, which may be red initially but will mature in about a year.
The patient will have drains in each breast for about a week and will need to avoid heavy lifting, driving, and strenuous activities for 2-3 weeks.
There is a 30% chance of reduced nipple sensation or no sensation after the surgery.
Follow-up: The patient will need to follow up with the office after the insurance approval and before the surgery to review the instructions and any last-minute questions.
